comments:
Summary
This short paper investigates two ways of learning matrix approximations of transitive verbs for compositional distributional semantics: a regression-based method that trains a plausibility classifier (using positive and negative SVO triples) and a distributional method that averages outer products of observed subject and object vectors. The two approaches are evaluated with several composition operators (Relational, Copy-subject, Verb-object) on two established datasets: GS2011 (verb disambiguation) and KS2013 (transitive sentence similarity). The main finding is that plausibility/regression training works better for verb disambiguation, while the purely distributional method performs better for sentence similarity; reg is advantageous at low dimensionalities while dist can leverage higher-dimensional vectors.

Strengths
- The paper addresses an important practical question in compositional distributional semantics: how to learn verb tensors/matrices and which training objective and sentence space suit different tasks. 
- The experimental setup compares several reasonable composition methods and two complementary learning approaches, and uses established benchmark datasets (GS2011, KS2013).
- The intuition offered for the observed differences (plausibility vs topic information) is plausible and useful for guiding future work.
- The paper explores dimensionality effects and reports practical considerations (training speed, storage).

Weaknesses and suggestions for improvement
- Limited methodological detail: while core formulas are given, several implementation details are missing or sparse in the provided text (e.g., exact hyperparameter choices, learning rate, stopping criteria beyond “10% validation”, number of epochs, regularisation selection process). Including these would improve reproducibility.
- Negative sampling heuristics: negatives are generated by replacing nouns with “maximally dissimilar” frequency-matched nouns. This may yield artificial negatives that are easy to classify or that do not reflect realistic near-miss confusions. A discussion/analysis of the quality of negatives (or experiments with alternative negative sampling strategies) would strengthen the claims about plausibility training.
- Evaluation reporting: the provided text references a Table 1 and Figure 1 but they are not shown here. It is unclear whether results include statistical significance testing or confidence intervals. The paper should report statistical significance and effect sizes to support claims about which method outperforms the other.
- Scope of experiments: experiments use Wikipedia SVOs with a cutoff and at most 300 triples per verb. This is reasonable but may bias against rarer senses. Broader evaluation (other corpora, different frequency cutoffs, more verbs) would increase confidence in generality.
- Analysis: the paper would benefit from more qualitative error analysis (example pairs where methods differ, and why), and investigation of when verb-object alone suffices versus when full relational composition is needed.
- Comparison to related work: the paper references prior tensor and matrix approaches but could more thoroughly position the contribution vis-a-vis recent alternatives (e.g., neural composition models), and comment on computational costs concretely.

Overall assessment
The paper presents a clear, focused empirical comparison with sensible hypotheses and useful findings for researchers working on compositional distributional semantics. The work is incremental rather than highly novel, but it provides practical insight (plausibility objective for disambiguation; distributional for topic-sensitive similarity) that will be of interest. Before publication in a venue with review, I would ask for added experimental details, significance testing, and more analysis of negative sampling and qualitative examples. For a short/initial study or workshop paper the current content is acceptable.

Concrete recommendations
- Add full experimental details and hyperparameter values; release code/data if possible.
- Report statistical significance for comparisons and provide full result tables/figures.
- Include qualitative examples and an analysis of negative sample quality.
- Explore additional datasets or ablations to test robustness to the training corpus and frequency cutoffs.

Recommendation (overall): accept as a short/workshop/poster contribution (score 7/10).
